h1Title: ANCA-Associated Vasculitis: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis & Treatment
heroOverview: ANCA-associated vasculitis (AAV) is a group of three autoimmune small-vessel vasculitides — granulomatosis with polyangiitis (GPA), microscopic polyangiitis (MPA), and eosinophilic granulomatosis with polyangiitis (EGPA) — defined by the presence of antineutrophil cytoplasmic antibodies (ANCA) and necrotising inflammation of small blood vessels. AAV affects approximately 20 per 100,000 persons in Europe and North America and is the most common form of systemic vasculitis requiring immunosuppression. Without treatment, severe GPA carries a 90% one-year mortality; with rituximab or cyclophosphamide induction followed by maintenance therapy, 5-year survival now exceeds 80%. The kidneys, lungs, and upper airways are the most frequently involved organs.

keyStats.prevalence: Approximately 20 per 100,000 persons in Europe and North America; GPA is most common (10-12/100,000), followed by MPA (4-8/100,000) and EGPA (2-4/100,000)
keyStats.demographics: GPA affects men and women equally; MPA has a slight male predominance; peak onset age 50-75 years; GPA has a second peak in young adults
keyStats.avgAge: Median diagnosis age 60-65 years for MPA and GPA; EGPA median age 40-50 years; rare under age 20
keyStats.globalCases: Approximately 700,000-1,000,000 people affected globally; incidence rising in part due to increased ANCA testing

definition: ANCA-associated vasculitis (ICD-10: M31.3 for GPA; M31.7 for MPA; M30.1 for EGPA) encompasses three related conditions unified by ANCA seropositivity and necrotising small-vessel inflammation without immune complex deposition (pauci-immune vasculitis). ANCA are directed against neutrophil granule proteins: anti-proteinase-3 (PR3-ANCA, c-ANCA) is predominantly associated with GPA; anti-myeloperoxidase (MPO-ANCA, p-ANCA) is associated with MPA and EGPA. Pathogenesis involves ANCA-mediated neutrophil activation, release of reactive oxygen species and proteases, and endothelial damage in small arteries, arterioles, capillaries, and venules. GPA (formerly Wegener's granulomatosis) is characterised by granulomatous inflammation of the upper airways (sinuses, nose, trachea) and lower respiratory tract combined with necrotising glomerulonephritis. MPA lacks the granulomatous component and upper respiratory involvement but causes aggressive glomerulonephritis and pulmonary capillaritis. EGPA (formerly Churg-Strauss syndrome) is uniquely associated with asthma, eosinophilia, and granulomatous eosinophilic inflammation of multiple organs. The 2022 ACR/EULAR classification criteria use a scoring system for each of the three subtypes. AAV is managed jointly by rheumatologists, nephrologists, and pulmonologists.

typesClassification.description: Previously called Wegener's granulomatosis. Granulomatous inflammation of the upper airways (persistent sinusitis, nasal septal perforation, saddle-nose deformity, subglottic stenosis) combined with necrotising glomerulonephritis and pulmonary nodules or infiltrates. PR3-ANCA positive in 75-90% of generalised disease. Most common AAV subtype.

Non-granulomatous necrotising vasculitis primarily affecting kidneys (necrotising glomerulonephritis) and lungs (pulmonary haemorrhage). MPO-ANCA positive in 60-70%. Lacks upper respiratory tract involvement. Often presents as rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis (RPGN) or pulmonary-renal syndrome.

Previously Churg-Strauss syndrome. Defined by asthma (invariably present), blood eosinophilia above 1,000/µL, and eosinophilic granulomatous vasculitis. MPO-ANCA positive in approximately 40%. Affects heart, peripheral nerves (mononeuritis multiplex), skin, and kidneys. Cardiac involvement is the leading cause of EGPA mortality.

primarySymptoms: Persistent, therapy-resistant sinusitis with purulent or bloody nasal discharge, nasal crusting, epistaxis, and progressive saddle-nose deformity due to cartilage destruction in GPA.
Haemoptysis or massive pulmonary haemorrhage (diffuse alveolar haemorrhage) from necrotising pulmonary capillaritis — a life-threatening emergency in MPA and GPA.
Rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis presenting as haematuria, red cell casts in urine, proteinuria, and precipitous creatinine rise over days to weeks.
Pulmonary nodules or infiltrates with or without cavitation on chest imaging, often asymptomatic but sometimes causing cough, dyspnoea, and haemoptysis in GPA.
Severe asthma with onset in adulthood (typically 30-50s) combined with marked peripheral eosinophilia (>1,000/µL) — the defining pre-vasculitic phase of EGPA.
Mononeuritis multiplex — sudden wrist drop, foot drop, or sensory loss in the distribution of a named nerve, reflecting vasculitic ischaemia of the vasa nervorum.
Subglottic or tracheal stenosis causing progressive stridor, hoarseness, and inspiratory dyspnoea in GPA — may require urgent airway intervention.
Constitutional symptoms — fever, weight loss (typically 5-15 kg), fatigue, and myalgia — present in the majority at diagnosis, often preceding organ-specific findings by weeks.
Scleritis, episcleritis, or orbital pseudotumour causing eye pain, redness, proptosis, and visual impairment — occur in up to 50% of GPA patients at some point.
Cutaneous purpura (palpable, lower limb predominant), livedo reticularis, skin nodules, and ulcers reflecting small-vessel involvement across all three AAV subtypes.
earlyWarningSigns: Persistent, unilateral sinusitis or otitis media unresponsive to repeated antibiotics in an adult over 50 — ENT referral and ANCA testing warranted if lasting more than 3 months.
New unexplained microscopic haematuria with red cell casts on urine microscopy — warrants immediate renal biopsy to exclude rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis.
Unexplained peripheral blood eosinophilia above 1,500/µL with new-onset asthma in an adult — EGPA should be excluded before attributing to allergy alone.
Mononeuritis multiplex (stepwise peripheral nerve deficits in a non-anatomical pattern) in a patient with systemic symptoms — vasculitis until proven otherwise.
emergencySigns: Massive haemoptysis or new-onset pulmonary infiltrates with haematuria — pulmonary-renal syndrome requires same-day ICU-level assessment, high-dose methylprednisolone, and urgent renal biopsy or bronchoscopy.
Creatinine rising more than 100 µmol/L per week with urinary red cell casts — rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis; delayed treatment leads to irreversible renal failure within days.
Stridor or acute airway compromise from tracheal or subglottic stenosis in a known GPA patient — requires urgent ENT or anaesthetic assessment.
New cardiac failure, arrhythmia, or cardiac arrest in an EGPA patient — eosinophilic myocarditis is the leading cause of EGPA mortality and requires urgent echocardiography and cardiology review.

causes.description: PR3-ANCA and MPO-ANCA directly activate primed neutrophils, causing degranulation and release of reactive oxygen species and proteolytic enzymes that damage the vascular endothelium. This is the central pathogenic mechanism shared by all three AAV subtypes and the rationale for B-cell depletion with rituximab.

GPA is strongly associated with HLA-DPB1*0401 and PR3-ANCA, while MPO-ANCA and MPA associate with HLA-DQ. The PTPN22 R620W polymorphism, which affects T-cell and B-cell signalling, is present in 20-30% of AAV patients versus 8% of controls. Genome-wide studies implicate SERPINA1 (alpha-1 antitrypsin) as a genetic risk factor for PR3-ANCA disease.

Staphylococcus aureus nasal carriage is significantly more prevalent in GPA patients than controls (70% vs 30%) and correlates with GPA relapse; prophylactic trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole reduces relapse by 40% in remission, supporting an infectious trigger. Silica dust exposure is associated with a 2-6-fold increased MPO-ANCA-positive vasculitis risk in occupationally exposed cohorts.

Hydralazine, propylthiouracil, minocycline, levamisole-adulterated cocaine, and anti-thyroid drugs can trigger MPO-ANCA-positive vasculitis as a class effect. Drug-induced AAV often remits after stopping the offending agent but requires immunosuppression for severe organ involvement.

causes.cause: T-cell dysregulation and impaired regulatory T-cell function
causes.description: Reduced Treg function and abnormal Th17 expansion amplify the ANCA-driven inflammatory cascade and contribute to granuloma formation in GPA. T-cell-directed therapies (abatacept, belimumab) are under investigation for treatment-resistant disease.

riskFactors.description: Incidence rises steeply with age; the majority of GPA and MPA diagnoses occur in the sixth and seventh decades. AAV is rare in children and young adults.

AAV prevalence is highest in Scandinavia (up to 30/100,000) and Northern Europe; GPA is rare in sub-Saharan Africa and South and East Asia, where MPO-ANCA MPA predominates. Genetic factors including HLA haplotype variation explain part of this pattern.

Occupational silica exposure (mining, quarrying, sandblasting) is associated with a 2-6-fold increased AAV risk in multiple case-control studies. Organic solvent and hydrocarbon exposure also associated with elevated MPO-ANCA disease.

Chronic S. aureus nasal colonisation is present in approximately 70% of GPA patients versus 30% of controls and is a major driver of GPA relapse. Decolonisation with trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole significantly reduces relapse rate.

Hydralazine and propylthiouracil are the most common drug triggers for MPO-ANCA vasculitis; levamisole-adulterated cocaine is an increasingly common cause in younger patients. Recognising and stopping the causative drug is the first step.

Pre-existing asthma, allergic rhinitis, and atopy are nearly universal in EGPA patients. The asthmatic phase typically begins 5-10 years before overt vasculitis onset. The mechanism likely involves eosinophil-driven tissue injury superimposed on a type 2 inflammatory background.

affectedDemographics: European and North American populations: highest incidence globally (20-30/100,000), particularly in Scandinavian countries and the UK.
Adults aged 60-75: GPA and MPA peak in the sixth and seventh decades; rare in children (paediatric AAV accounts for less than 5% of all AAV).
EGPA: younger median age (40-50) than GPA/MPA; strong atopic history in nearly all affected patients; cardiac complications more frequent in ANCA-negative EGPA.
Occupational cohorts: silica-exposed workers (mining, construction, quarrying) show 2-6-fold elevated AAV risk compared with unexposed controls.
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis of AAV requires integration of clinical features, ANCA serology, tissue biopsy, and radiology. ANCA testing by ELISA for PR3-ANCA and MPO-ANCA (the 2017 international consensus recommendation, which replaced indirect immunofluorescence as the first-line test) is the critical serological step. PR3-ANCA sensitivity for active generalised GPA is 75-90%; MPO-ANCA sensitivity for MPA is 60-70%; ANCA may be negative in up to 40% of EGPA and limited GPA. Renal biopsy, when the kidneys are involved, is the gold standard — demonstrating focal, segmental, necrotising glomerulonephritis with few immune deposits (pauci-immune) is pathognomonic. Tissue biopsy from affected sites (nasal mucosa, lung, skin, nerve) shows necrotising granulomatous inflammation in GPA and eosinophilic granulomatous inflammation in EGPA. Chest CT characteristically shows bilateral pulmonary nodules or cavities in GPA and bilateral ground-glass opacification (diffuse alveolar haemorrhage) in MPA and GPA with pulmonary haemorrhage. Urine microscopy for red cell casts is mandatory at every assessment. The 2022 ACR/EULAR classification criteria use weighted scoring systems for GPA, MPA, and EGPA to standardise clinical trial enrolment, though diagnosis remains clinical. The Birmingham Vasculitis Activity Score (BVAS) and Vasculitis Damage Index (VDI) quantify disease activity and accumulated damage for treatment decisions and monitoring.

diagnosticTests.purpose: The primary serological test for AAV; PR3-ANCA is strongly associated with GPA, MPO-ANCA with MPA and EGPA
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard blood draw; results within 3-5 working days from specialist immunology laboratories. High-positive PR3- or MPO-ANCA in the right clinical context is highly specific for AAV (>95% specificity at high titres).

diagnosticTests.test: Renal biopsy with light and immunofluorescence microscopy
diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms pauci-immune necrotising glomerulonephritis; the gold-standard diagnostic test for renal AAV; guides prognosis and decisions about dialysis avoidance
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Day-case or inpatient procedure under ultrasound guidance using a core-needle biopsy under local anaesthetic. Mild discomfort and brief haematuria afterwards; results within 3-7 working days. Requires near-normal coagulation.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects pulmonary nodules, cavities, ground-glass opacification (diffuse alveolar haemorrhage), and bronchial stenosis
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A 15-20 minute scan, lying still. No injection needed for a standard HRCT; contrast added if pulmonary embolism is also suspected. Results within 24-48 hours.

diagnosticTests.test: Urine microscopy for red cell casts and proteinuria
diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects active glomerulonephritis; red cell casts are the most specific bedside marker of glomerular bleeding
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Fresh urine sample (midstream catch) examined within 2 hours of collection. Results from a skilled laboratory technician within 2-4 hours. Should be performed at every clinical assessment during active disease.

diagnosticTests.test: Full blood count, CRP, creatinine, eGFR
diagnosticTests.purpose: Monitors disease activity (CRP, neutrophil count) and renal function; eosinophilia above 1,500/µL with asthma strongly suggests EGPA
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw; results same day. eGFR trajectory and CRP are the primary monitoring tools between specialist appointments.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms diffuse alveolar haemorrhage when haemoptysis and infiltrates are present; BAL fluid showing progressively bloody return across three aliquots is diagnostic
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Performed under sedation; a bronchoscope is passed through the airways and saline is instilled and retrieved from affected lung segments. Takes 30-45 minutes. Mild throat soreness and cough for 24 hours afterwards.

treatmentOverview: AAV treatment follows the 2022 ACR/EULAR guidelines in two phases: induction of remission and maintenance. For severe AAV (organ- or life-threatening disease — RPGN, diffuse alveolar haemorrhage, threatened vision), high-dose glucocorticoids combined with either rituximab (anti-CD20, 375 mg/m² weekly × 4 doses or 1g × 2 doses 2 weeks apart) or cyclophosphamide (IV pulse 15 mg/kg every 2-3 weeks, or oral 2 mg/kg/day) are first-line induction. The 2010 RAVE trial and 2021 ADVOCATE trial established rituximab as non-inferior to cyclophosphamide for GPA induction and superior for PR3-ANCA-positive relapsing disease. Plasma exchange was previously recommended for severe alveolar haemorrhage or dialysis-dependent RPGN but the PEXIVAS RCT (2020) demonstrated no mortality or end-stage renal disease benefit and is no longer routinely recommended. After achieving remission (typically 3-6 months), maintenance with rituximab (500 mg every 6 months) or azathioprine (2 mg/kg/day) continues for at least 24 months; rituximab maintenance is superior to azathioprine for PR3-ANCA GPA. For non-severe (no vital organ involvement) AAV, methotrexate-based induction is an alternative. Avacopan (a C5a receptor inhibitor, FDA/EMA approved 2021) can replace high-dose oral prednisolone tapering, reducing glucocorticoid exposure. EGPA with poor prognostic factors uses the same induction strategy; mepolizumab (anti-IL-5 monoclonal antibody) reduces EGPA relapse frequency and oral glucocorticoid dose in ANCA-negative and ANCA-positive patients in relapsing disease.

medicalTreatments.name: Rituximab (375 mg/m² IV × 4 weekly doses or 1g × 2 doses 14 days apart)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line induction for severe GPA, MPA, and relapsing PR3-ANCA AAV. Anti-CD20 monoclonal antibody depletes B cells, reducing ANCA production. Also used for maintenance: 500 mg IV every 6 months for at least 24 months after remission.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Non-inferior to cyclophosphamide for remission induction (64% remission vs 53% cyclophosphamide at 6 months, RAVE trial); superior to azathioprine for relapse prevention in PR3-ANCA GPA

medicalTreatments.name: Cyclophosphamide IV (15 mg/kg per pulse, every 2-3 weeks × 6-9 pulses)
medicalTreatments.description: Alternative induction agent for severe AAV when rituximab is unavailable or contraindicated; particularly used in resource-limited settings and MPA with dialysis dependency. Bladder protective hydration and mesna required.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Remission in 53-75% of severe AAV within 6 months in RAVE and CYCLOPS trials; equivalent efficacy to oral regimen with fewer side effects

medicalTreatments.name: High-dose methylprednisolone (500-1,000 mg IV × 3 days) followed by oral prednisolone taper
medicalTreatments.description: Initial pulse corticosteroids are used for rapidly progressive or immediately life-threatening AAV. Oral prednisolone tapered from 1 mg/kg/day, targeting cessation or minimal dose by 6 months. Represents the greatest source of AAV morbidity from infection and metabolic complications.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Critical for rapid disease control; all current induction regimens incorporate initial glucocorticoids

medicalTreatments.name: Avacopan (30 mg twice daily orally)
medicalTreatments.description: C5a receptor inhibitor approved in 2021 (FDA, EMA) as a partial substitute for oral glucocorticoids during induction. In the ADVOCATE trial, avacopan was non-inferior to prednisolone taper at week 26 and superior at week 52 for sustained remission.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Sustained remission at week 52 in 65.7% (avacopan) vs 54.9% (prednisone); significantly reduced glucocorticoid-related adverse events

medicalTreatments.name: Azathioprine (2 mg/kg/day orally)
medicalTreatments.description: Alternative maintenance agent after cyclophosphamide induction, particularly for MPO-ANCA MPA. Less effective than rituximab maintenance for PR3-ANCA GPA relapse prevention. Requires TPMT genotyping before initiation to avoid life-threatening myelosuppression.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: 18-month major relapse rate 15% (azathioprine) vs 11% (rituximab) in MAINRITSAN; acceptable second-line maintenance option

medicalTreatments.name: Mepolizumab (300 mg SC every 4 weeks)
medicalTreatments.description: Anti-IL-5 monoclonal antibody approved (2017) for relapsing or refractory EGPA. Reduces eosinophil-driven disease activity and oral prednisolone requirements without replacing induction therapy for severe EGPA.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: 56% of EGPA patients achieved remission (ANCA and ANCA-negative combined) vs 3% placebo in the MIRRA trial; 50% achieved prednisolone dose ≤4 mg/day

surgicalOptions.name: Subglottic/tracheal dilation or stenting
surgicalOptions.description: For GPA-related subglottic or tracheal stenosis causing stridor or respiratory compromise. Endoscopic balloon dilation, laser resection, or stenting is used; topical intralesional corticosteroid injection is often given concurrently. Disease activity must be controlled systemically.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Symptomatic improvement in 70-85% after initial dilation; multiple procedures often required as recurrence rate is approximately 40-60% over 2 years

surgicalOptions.name: Renal replacement therapy (haemodialysis or peritoneal dialysis)
surgicalOptions.description: Initiated for AAV-associated end-stage renal disease (eGFR <15 mL/min with symptoms). Renal transplantation is appropriate after sustained remission; 5-year graft survival in AAV patients is comparable to other autoimmune kidney diseases.
surgicalOptions.successRate: 50-65% of dialysis-dependent AAV patients with renal biopsy showing >50% viable glomeruli recover some renal function with aggressive immunosuppression

alternativeTreatments.name: Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole (co-trimoxazole) for GPA remission maintenance
alternativeTreatments.description: A 1996 RCT (Stegeman et al. NEJM, PMID 8551580) showed that co-trimoxazole reduced GPA relapse rate by 40% compared with placebo in patients in remission, likely through Staphylococcus aureus decolonisation. Used adjunctively in GPA maintenance and also provides Pneumocystis jirovecii prophylaxis.

whySeeSpecialist: AAV is a rare, rapidly destructive autoimmune disease that requires specialist rheumatology or nephrology input for diagnosis and management. Delayed immunosuppression in RPGN or diffuse alveolar haemorrhage leads to irreversible end-organ failure; a suspicion of AAV warrants same-day specialist referral.
doctorSelectionGuide: Seek a rheumatologist or nephrologist at a centre with specific AAV expertise, access to ANCA testing by ELISA, specialist renal biopsy services, and experience with rituximab and cyclophosphamide protocols. Multidisciplinary teams including ENT, pulmonology, and ophthalmology are needed for GPA with multi-organ involvement.
hospitalCriteria: Rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis with rising creatinine — inpatient assessment and urgent renal biopsy
Diffuse alveolar haemorrhage or haemoptysis requiring ICU-level monitoring
Initiation of IV cyclophosphamide or rituximab induction requiring monitoring for infusion reactions
EGPA with cardiac involvement (eosinophilic myocarditis, arrhythmia)
keyFacilities: Specialist vasculitis clinics within academic rheumatology or nephrology departments
Centres with access to ANCA ELISA testing and renal biopsy services
Multidisciplinary teams including ENT, pulmonology, ophthalmology, and cardiology
ICU with nephrological support for life-threatening presentations
preventionStrategies: Avoid occupational silica and solvent exposure — use respiratory protection in high-risk industries (mining, sandblasting, quarrying) to reduce the 2-6-fold elevated AAV risk.
Report any new unexplained nasal, pulmonary, or renal symptoms promptly in patients with a first-degree relative with AAV — earlier diagnosis limits organ damage.
Stop offending drugs (hydralazine, propylthiouracil, levamisole-containing cocaine) immediately in patients developing ANCA positivity or vasculitic symptoms.
Eradicate Staphylococcus aureus nasal carriage in GPA patients using intranasal mupirocin and co-trimoxazole prophylaxis during remission to reduce relapse risk by approximately 40%.

Vaccination against Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia with co-trimoxazole 960 mg three times weekly is mandatory during and after rituximab or cyclophosphamide induction to prevent opportunistic infection.
lifestyleModifications: Attend specialist clinic follow-up every 3 months during the first 2 years of treatment; ANCA titre, CRP, and urine microscopy guide dose adjustments and detect relapse before organ damage escalates.
Monitor blood pressure at home weekly — AAV nephritis and glucocorticoid treatment both cause hypertension that must be treated aggressively to protect residual renal function.
Take bone protection (calcium, vitamin D, bisphosphonate) from the start of glucocorticoid treatment — prolonged prednisolone causes vertebral fractures in 20-30% of AAV patients without prophylaxis.
Avoid live vaccines during immunosuppressive therapy; update pneumococcal, influenza, and COVID-19 vaccines before or during low-intensity immunosuppression phases.
Avoid smoking — smoking worsens pulmonary involvement, cardiovascular risk from glucocorticoids, and infection susceptibility.
Register with the European Vasculitis Society (EUVAS) patient registry or a national vasculitis registry to contribute longitudinal outcome data and receive updates on clinical trials.

recommended: Low-salt diet (<2,000 mg sodium/day) to mitigate glucocorticoid-related hypertension and oedema
Calcium-rich foods (dairy, fortified plant-based alternatives, leafy greens) alongside vitamin D supplementation to counteract glucocorticoid-induced bone loss
High-protein diet during active disease and glucocorticoid induction to prevent muscle wasting and catabolism
Heart-healthy diet (Mediterranean pattern) to reduce the markedly elevated cardiovascular risk from chronic glucocorticoid exposure
avoid: High-sodium processed foods — exacerbate hypertension and fluid retention from glucocorticoids
Alcohol during cyclophosphamide or methotrexate treatment — increases hepatotoxicity risk and interacts with antiemetics
Grapefruit and grapefruit juice during azathioprine therapy — does not directly interact but is associated with metabolic changes
Excess simple sugars — prolonged glucocorticoid treatment causes steroid diabetes in 20-30% of patients; a low-glycaemic-index diet reduces risk

exerciseGuidelines: Moderate exercise is encouraged during remission to counter glucocorticoid-related muscle weakness, osteoporosis, and cardiovascular risk. Weight-bearing exercise (walking, cycling, resistance training) should begin at low intensity during maintenance therapy and increase as tolerated. Patients with renal impairment should discuss intensity targets with their nephrologist. Exercise is paused during active flares or serious infection.
dailyManagement: Take oral prednisolone and immunosuppressant doses at the same time each day with food; never stop glucocorticoids abruptly.
Check urine monthly with a dipstick for blood and protein between specialist appointments — early haematuria precedes clinical flare and should trigger urgent contact with the vasculitis team.
Monitor blood pressure at home 2-3 times per week; systolic above 140 mmHg persistently should be reported and treated.
Carry a treatment card or alert bracelet identifying ANCA vasculitis and current immunosuppressants in case of emergency or intercurrent illness.
Report any fever, new respiratory symptoms, or unexplained bruising immediately — infection and leucopenia are the most common serious treatment complications.
Take co-trimoxazole 960 mg three times weekly or pentamidine inhaler if unable to tolerate co-trimoxazole throughout rituximab or cyclophosphamide therapy.
prognosis: With contemporary rituximab-based treatment, 5-year survival for AAV exceeds 80%, compared with fewer than 10% in the pre-immunosuppression era. Complete remission is achieved in 60-80% of patients by 6 months. However, relapse rates remain high: 30-50% of GPA patients relapse within 5 years, particularly those with PR3-ANCA positivity and persistent ANCA at remission. End-stage renal disease develops in 20-30% of patients over 10 years, with baseline creatinine and the extent of glomerular scarring on biopsy being the strongest predictors. Infection is the leading cause of early mortality, responsible for 20-30% of AAV deaths in the first year — primarily reflecting glucocorticoid toxicity. Long-term morbidity includes glucocorticoid-related osteoporosis, diabetes, and cataracts; cardiovascular events in the medium term from accelerated atherosclerosis; and persistent renal impairment. EGPA has better overall survival than GPA and MPA (5-year survival 90-95%) but cardiac involvement at diagnosis significantly worsens prognosis.
recoveryTimeline: Remission induction typically takes 3-6 months from start of rituximab or cyclophosphamide. eGFR recovery, if it occurs, is most rapid in the first 3-6 months after immunosuppression. Maintenance therapy continues for minimum 24 months after remission. ANCA titres (PR3 or MPO) normalise over 6-12 months in most patients achieving complete remission and can serve as relapse biomarkers.
complications: End-stage renal disease — develops in 20-30% of AAV patients over 10 years; strongly predicted by creatinine at diagnosis and the proportion of globally sclerosed glomeruli on biopsy.

Severe infections (Pneumocystis jirovecii, invasive aspergillosis, CMV) — responsible for 20-30% of first-year AAV deaths; all patients on rituximab or cyclophosphamide require Pneumocystis prophylaxis.
Glucocorticoid toxicity — vertebral fractures (20-30%), steroid diabetes (20-30%), avascular necrosis, cataracts, and Cushingoid changes accumulate with cumulative dose over years.
Relapse — 30-50% of PR3-ANCA GPA patients relapse within 5 years; each relapse adds further organ damage and cumulative immunosuppressive toxicity.
Hearing loss and cranial neuropathy in GPA — subacute sensorineural hearing loss and cranial nerve palsies from granulomatous inflammation are poorly reversible once established.

Bladder cancer from cyclophosphamide — lifetime haemorrhagic cystitis risk 2-10% and bladder cancer risk 5-fold elevated with cumulative oral cyclophosphamide doses above 35 g; rituximab induction has largely reduced this risk.

supportResources.name: Vasculitis UK
supportResources.description: UK patient charity providing helpline, peer support groups, and evidence-based information for all AAV subtypes.

supportResources.name: Vasculitis Foundation
supportResources.description: US-based patient organisation with disease guides, physician directories, and research funding information for AAV and related conditions.

supportResources.name: ACR/EULAR 2022 Classification Criteria for GPA, MPA, and EGPA
supportResources.description: ACR guideline hub with the 2022 AAV classification criteria and treatment recommendations.

supportResources.name: EUVAS (European Vasculitis Society)
supportResources.description: Pan-European vasculitis research network with patient information, trial registries, and clinical guidelines.

confusedWithConditions.name: Goodpasture Syndrome
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Goodpasture syndrome (anti-GBM disease) also presents as pulmonary-renal syndrome with haemoptysis and glomerulonephritis, but is caused by anti-glomerular basement membrane antibodies (anti-GBM), not ANCA. Anti-GBM disease shows linear IgG deposits on renal biopsy (versus pauci-immune in AAV) and anti-GBM antibodies in serum. Both can coexist (double-positivity in 10-30% of anti-GBM cases).

confusedWithConditions.name: Systemic Lupus Erythematosus
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: SLE can cause nephritis, pulmonary haemorrhage, and vasculitis, but has positive ANA, dsDNA, and low complement alongside immune complex deposits on renal biopsy (full-house immunofluorescence) — the opposite of pauci-immune AAV. SLE treatment prioritises hydroxychloroquine and mycophenolate rather than rituximab induction.

confusedWithConditions.name: Rheumatoid Arthritis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Rheumatoid vasculitis occurs in severe, long-standing RA and can mimic AAV with mononeuritis multiplex and skin vasculitis. Distinguishing features are the RA joint history, rheumatoid factor and anti-CCP positivity, and absence of ANCA. Glomerulonephritis and pulmonary haemorrhage are uncommon in rheumatoid vasculitis.

confusedWithConditions.name: IgA Nephropathy
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Both IgA nephropathy and AAV cause haematuria and proteinuria, but IgA nephropathy shows mesangial IgA deposits on biopsy (immune-complex positive), is ANCA negative, and presents more slowly without the constitutional features or upper respiratory tract granulomatous involvement of GPA.

relatedConditions.name: Goodpasture Syndrome
relatedConditions.relevance: Both cause pulmonary-renal syndrome; anti-GBM antibodies and ANCA can co-exist in double-positive disease requiring combined treatment.

relatedConditions.name: Systemic Lupus Erythematosus
relatedConditions.relevance: Another systemic autoimmune disease causing nephritis and vasculitis, sharing several clinical features with AAV but managed with distinct therapeutic strategies.

relatedConditions.name: Giant Cell Arteritis
relatedConditions.relevance: Another systemic vasculitis managed by rheumatologists; affects large vessels in contrast to AAV's small-vessel predilection and rarely produces ANCA seropositivity.

relatedConditions.name: IgA Nephropathy
relatedConditions.relevance: Key renal differential for haematuria and crescentic glomerulonephritis; distinguished from AAV by immune-complex deposits on biopsy and ANCA negativity.

faqs.question: What is ANCA-associated vasculitis in simple terms?
faqs.answer: ANCA-associated vasculitis is an autoimmune disease where antibodies (ANCA) cause inflammation and damage to small blood vessels throughout the body, most severely affecting the kidneys, lungs, and upper airways. It includes three subtypes: GPA, MPA, and EGPA. Without treatment it is life-threatening, but modern immunosuppression achieves remission in most patients.

faqs.question: What are the three types of ANCA vasculitis?
faqs.answer: The three types are granulomatosis with polyangiitis (GPA, formerly Wegener's), microscopic polyangiitis (MPA), and eosinophilic granulomatosis with polyangiitis (EGPA, formerly Churg-Strauss). GPA causes upper airway granulomas and kidney disease; MPA causes kidney and lung damage without granulomas; EGPA is defined by severe asthma and eosinophilia.

faqs.question: How is ANCA vasculitis diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis combines ANCA serology (PR3-ANCA or MPO-ANCA by ELISA), tissue biopsy (typically renal or nasal), and radiology (chest CT). Renal biopsy showing pauci-immune necrotising glomerulonephritis is diagnostic. Urine microscopy for red cell casts and rising creatinine signal active kidney disease requiring urgent assessment.

faqs.question: What is the difference between PR3-ANCA and MPO-ANCA?
faqs.answer: PR3-ANCA (c-ANCA) is directed against proteinase-3 and is associated with GPA in 75-90% of active generalised cases. MPO-ANCA (p-ANCA) targets myeloperoxidase and is associated with MPA and EGPA. PR3-ANCA GPA has a higher relapse rate and benefits more from rituximab maintenance than MPO-ANCA MPA.

faqs.question: What is the first-line treatment for ANCA vasculitis?
faqs.answer: For severe AAV, the 2022 ACR/EULAR guidelines recommend rituximab or cyclophosphamide combined with high-dose glucocorticoids for induction of remission. Rituximab is preferred for GPA and relapsing PR3-ANCA disease. Avacopan (2021) can partially replace high-dose oral prednisolone, reducing steroid toxicity.

faqs.question: Can ANCA vasculitis be cured?
faqs.answer: AAV is not cured but is controlled with treatment. Complete remission is achieved in 60-80% of patients by 6 months of induction therapy. However, relapses occur in 30-50% of GPA patients within 5 years, and ongoing maintenance immunosuppression is required for at least 24 months after remission.

faqs.question: Does ANCA vasculitis cause kidney failure?
faqs.answer: Yes. End-stage renal disease develops in 20-30% of AAV patients over 10 years, particularly those presenting with high creatinine or extensive glomerular scarring on biopsy. Early diagnosis and aggressive immunosuppression are the key interventions to preserve kidney function.

faqs.question: What is Wegener's granulomatosis?
faqs.answer: Wegener's granulomatosis is the former name for granulomatosis with polyangiitis (GPA), renamed in 2011 due to the discoverer's Nazi affiliations. GPA is the most common ANCA vasculitis subtype, characterised by destructive granulomatous inflammation of the upper airways and kidneys, with PR3-ANCA positivity in up to 90% of cases.

faqs.question: Is ANCA vasculitis hereditary?
faqs.answer: AAV is not directly inherited but has significant genetic risk factors, including HLA-DPB1 and HLA-DQ haplotypes and the PTPN22 polymorphism. First-degree relatives carry a modestly elevated risk, but AAV is not considered a single-gene hereditary disorder. Environmental factors (silica, S. aureus) interact with genetic susceptibility.

faqs.question: What are the early signs of ANCA vasculitis?
faqs.answer: Early signs include persistent sinusitis unresponsive to antibiotics, bloody nasal crusting, unexplained microscopic haematuria, fatigue and weight loss, or new-onset asthma with high eosinophils. Mononeuritis multiplex (sudden foot or wrist drop) and scleritis are also early features. ANCA testing should be done promptly when these features cluster.

faqs.question: What is avacopan used for in AAV?
faqs.answer: Avacopan is a C5a receptor inhibitor approved in 2021 for ANCA vasculitis. It can partially replace high-dose oral prednisolone in the induction phase, reducing glucocorticoid-related toxicity such as infections, diabetes, and bone loss. The ADVOCATE trial showed avacopan was non-inferior to prednisolone at 26 weeks and superior at 52 weeks for sustained remission.

faqs.question: How often does ANCA vasculitis relapse?
faqs.answer: Relapse rates are highest in GPA with PR3-ANCA positivity: approximately 30-50% within 5 years. MPO-ANCA MPA has lower relapse rates (15-20% at 5 years). Rituximab maintenance every 6 months significantly reduces relapse compared with azathioprine in PR3-ANCA GPA.

faqs.question: Can ANCA vasculitis affect the lungs?
faqs.answer: Yes. Pulmonary involvement is common in GPA (cavitating nodules, infiltrates) and MPA (pulmonary capillaritis causing diffuse alveolar haemorrhage). Haemoptysis or new pulmonary infiltrates with haematuria is a pulmonary-renal syndrome requiring emergency assessment.

faqs.question: What blood tests monitor ANCA vasculitis?
faqs.answer: Key monitoring tests include ANCA titres (rising PR3 or MPO-ANCA predicts relapse in GPA), serum creatinine and eGFR, CRP, full blood count for cytopenias, and urine dipstick with microscopy for red cell casts. Clinic frequency is typically every 3 months during the first 2 years.

faqs.question: What is the life expectancy for ANCA vasculitis?
faqs.answer: With modern rituximab-based treatment, 5-year survival exceeds 80%. Early mortality (first year) is primarily from infection related to immunosuppression and from uncontrolled organ failure at presentation. Long-term survivors face elevated cardiovascular risk and the consequences of chronic kidney disease.
